subject: Physics
q: If the collision frequency of hydrogen molecules in a closed chamber at $27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is $\mathrm{Z}$, then the collision frequency of the same system at $127^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is :
question: If the collision frequency of hydrogen molecules in a closed chamber at $27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is $\mathrm{Z}$, then the collision frequency of the same system at $127^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is :
solution:
Assuming mean free path constant.
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{f} \propto \mathrm{V} \propto \sqrt{\mathrm{T}} \\ & \frac{\mathrm{f}_1}{\mathrm{f}_2}=\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{T}_1}{\mathrm{~T}_2}}=\sqrt{\frac{300}{400}} \\ & \mathrm{f}_2=\sqrt{\frac{4}{3}}=\mathrm{f}_1=\frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \mathrm{Z}\end{aligned}$

question: Two spherical soap bubbles of radii <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>r</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>r</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> in vacuum combine under isothermal conditions. The resulting bubble has a radius equal to:
solution:

n1+n2=n3

P1 V1+P2 V2=P3 V

4 Sr143πr13+4 Sr243πr23=4 Sr343πr33

r12+r22=r32

r3=r12+r22

subject: Physics
q: At <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>300</mn><mo> </mo><mi>K</mi></math>, the rms speed of oxygen molecules is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msqrt><mfrac><mrow><mi>α</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow><mi>α</mi></mfrac></msqrt></math> times to that of its average speed in the gas. Then, the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math> will be (use <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">π</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mn>22</mn><mn>7</mn></mfrac></math>)
question: At <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>300</mn><mo> </mo><mi>K</mi></math>, the rms speed of oxygen molecules is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msqrt><mfrac><mrow><mi>α</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow><mi>α</mi></mfrac></msqrt></math> times to that of its average speed in the gas. Then, the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math> will be (use <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">π</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mn>22</mn><mn>7</mn></mfrac></math>)
solution:
RMS speed is given by, vrms=3RTM and average speed is given by, vavg=8πRTM.

As per question,

vrms=α+5αvavg

⇒3RTM=α+5α8πRTM

⇒3=α+5α×8227

⇒α=28

subject: Physics
q:
Match the $\mathrm {List-I}$ with $\mathrm {List-II}$
$\begin{array}{|l|l|l|l|}\hline & \text{List-I} & & \text{List-II} \\ \hline \text{A.} & \text{Triatomic rigid gas} & \text{I.} & \frac{\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{P}}}{\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{V}}}=\frac{5}{3} \\ \hline \text{B.} & \begin{array}{l} \text{Diatomic non-rigid} \\ \text{gas} \end{array} & \text{II.} & \frac{\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{P}}}{\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{V}}}=\frac{7}{5} \\ \hline \text{C.} & \text{Monoatomic gas} & \text{III.} & \frac{\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{P}}}{\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{V}}}=\frac{4}{3} \\ \hline \text{D.} & \text{Diatomic rigid gas} & \text{IV }& \frac{\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{P}}}{\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{V}}}=\frac{9}{7} \\ \hline\end{array}$
Choose the $\mathrm {correct}$ answer from the options given below :
question:
Match the $\mathrm {List-I}$ with $\mathrm {List-II}$
$\begin{array}{|l|l|l|l|}\hline & \text{List-I} & & \text{List-II} \\ \hline \text{A.} & \text{Triatomic rigid gas} & \text{I.} & \frac{\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{P}}}{\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{V}}}=\frac{5}{3} \\ \hline \text{B.} & \begin{array}{l} \text{Diatomic non-rigid} \\ \text{gas} \end{array} & \text{II.} & \frac{\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{P}}}{\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{V}}}=\frac{7}{5} \\ \hline \text{C.} & \text{Monoatomic gas} & \text{III.} & \frac{\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{P}}}{\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{V}}}=\frac{4}{3} \\ \hline \text{D.} & \text{Diatomic rigid gas} & \text{IV }& \frac{\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{P}}}{\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{V}}}=\frac{9}{7} \\ \hline\end{array}$
Choose the $\mathrm {correct}$ answer from the options given below :
solution:
$\gamma=1+\frac{2}{\mathrm{f}}$
$f=6$, Triatomic rigid gas
$\mathrm{f}=7$, Diatomic non-rigid gas
$f=5$, Diatomic rigid gas
$\mathrm{f}=3$, monoatomic rigid gas
$\gamma=1+\frac{2}{6}=\frac{4}{3}$ (Triatomic)
$\gamma=1+\frac{2}{7}=\frac{9}{7}($ Diatomic, non-rigid $)$
$\gamma=1+\frac{2}{5}=\frac{7}{5}($ Diatomic, rigid $)$
$\gamma=1+\frac{2}{3}=\frac{5}{3}($ Monoatomic, rigid $)$
A-III, B-IV, C-I, D-II

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements:
Statement I: The temperature of a gas is -73 °C. When the gas is heated to 527 °C, the root mean square speed of the molecules is doubled.
Statement II : The product of pressure and volume of an ideal gas will be equal to translational kinetic energy of the molecules.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below:
question:
Given below are two statements:
Statement I: The temperature of a gas is -73 °C. When the gas is heated to 527 °C, the root mean square speed of the molecules is doubled.
Statement II : The product of pressure and volume of an ideal gas will be equal to translational kinetic energy of the molecules.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below:
options: Both Statement I and Statement II are true
Statement I is true but Statement II is false
Both Statement I and Statement II are false
Statement I is false but Statement II is true
solution:
Given:

T1=-73 °C=-73+273 K=200 K

and T2=527°C=527+273 K=800 K

Therefore,

Vrms, 1Vrms, 2=3RT1M3RT2M=T1T2

=200800=12

Vrms, 2=2 Vrms, 1

Hence,

Statement-I is true.

Now,

PV=nRT

But translational KE=32nRT

Hence, Statement-II is false.

q: A bullet of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>gram</mi></math>, travelling with a speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>210</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> strikes a fixed wooden target. One half of its kinetic energy is converted into heat in the wood. The rise of temperature of the bullet if the specific heat of its material is  <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>030</mn><mo> </mo><msup><mfenced><mrow><mi>gram</mi><mo> </mo><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></mrow></mfenced><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>calorie</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><mo>.2</mo><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>7</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi>ergs</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> close to :
question: A bullet of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>gram</mi></math>, travelling with a speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>210</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> strikes a fixed wooden target. One half of its kinetic energy is converted into heat in the wood. The rise of temperature of the bullet if the specific heat of its material is  <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>030</mn><mo> </mo><msup><mfenced><mrow><mi>gram</mi><mo> </mo><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></mrow></mfenced><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>calorie</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><mo>.2</mo><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>7</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi>ergs</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> close to :
solution:
As per given condition Half of kinetic energy is transferred into heat energy and raises the temperature of bullet

12×12mv2=msΔT

Δt=V24s

=210×2104×4.2×0.3×1000=87.5°C

subject: Physics
q: At which temperature the r.m.s. velocity of a hydrogen molecule equal to that of an oxygen molecule at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>47</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> ?
question: At which temperature the r.m.s. velocity of a hydrogen molecule equal to that of an oxygen molecule at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>47</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> ?
solution:
The r.m.s velocity of the molecule of a gas of molecular weight M at Kelvin temperature T is given by, Vrms=3RTM.

Let MO and MH are molecular weight of oxygen and hydrogen and TO and TH the corresponding Kelvin temperature at which Vr.m.s is same for both,

Vr.m.s=3RTOMO=3RTHMH. Hence TOMO=THMH

TO=273°C+47°C=320 K, 

MO=32, 

MH=2⇒TH=232×320=20 K

q: The temperature of a gas is $-78^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and the average translational kinetic energy of its molecules is $\mathrm{K}$. The temperature at which the average translational kinetic energy of the molecules of the same gas becomes $2 \mathrm{~K}$ is :
question: The temperature of a gas is $-78^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and the average translational kinetic energy of its molecules is $\mathrm{K}$. The temperature at which the average translational kinetic energy of the molecules of the same gas becomes $2 \mathrm{~K}$ is :
solution:
$\begin{aligned}& \mathrm{K} . \mathrm{E}=\frac{\mathrm{nf}_1 \mathrm{RT}}{2} \\& \mathrm{~T}_{\mathrm{i}}=-78^{\circ} \mathrm{C} \rightarrow 273+\left[-78^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\right]=195 \mathrm{~K}
\end{aligned}$
K.E $\alpha \mathrm{T}$
To double the K.E energy temp also become double
$\begin{aligned}& \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{f}}=390 \mathrm{~K} \\
& \mathrm{~T}_{\mathrm{f}}=117^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\end{aligned}$

If the partition is removed without doing any work on the gas, the final equilibrium temperature of the gas in the container will be
question: An insulated container of gas has two chambers separated by an insulating partition. One of the chambers has volume $V_1$ and contains ideal gas at pressure $P_1$ and temperature $T_1$. The other chamber has volume $V_2$ and contains ideal gas at pressure $P_2$ and temperature $T_2$. If the partition is removed without doing any work on the gas, the final equilibrium temperature of the gas in the container will be
options: $\frac{T_1 T_2\left(P_1 V_1+P_2 V_2\right)}{P_1 V_1 T_2+P_2 V_2 T_1}$
$\frac{T_1 T_2\left(P_1 V_1+P_2 V_2\right)}{P_1 V_1 T_1+P_2 V_2 T_2}$

The helium and argon are put in the flask at the same room temperature ( 300 K). The ratio of average kinetic energies (per molecule) of helium and argon is :
(Give : Molar mass of helium $=4 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol}$, Molar mass of argon $=40 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol}$)
question:
The helium and argon are put in the flask at the same room temperature ( 300 K). The ratio of average kinetic energies (per molecule) of helium and argon is :
(Give : Molar mass of helium $=4 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol}$, Molar mass of argon $=40 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol}$)
solution:
$K. E=\frac{f}{2} K T$
For He and $\mathrm{Ar} \mathrm{f}=3$
$\frac{\mathrm{K} \cdot \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{He}}}{\mathrm{~K} \cdot \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{Ar}}}=\frac{1}{1}$

subject: Physics
q: The pressure <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi>P</mi><mo>)</mo></math> and temperature <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi>T</mi><mo>)</mo></math> relationship of an ideal gas obeys the equation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><msup><mi>T</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo></math> constant. The volume expansion coefficient of the gas will be :
question: The pressure <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi>P</mi><mo>)</mo></math> and temperature <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi>T</mi><mo>)</mo></math> relationship of an ideal gas obeys the equation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><msup><mi>T</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo></math> constant. The volume expansion coefficient of the gas will be :
solution:
Given here,

PT2=constant    ...1

Using ideal gas equation, 

PV=nRT

We have, P=nRTV

Putting in equation 1,

PT2=nRTV×T2=constant

⇒T3=KV   ...2

Differentiating the equation with respect to T, 

So, ddTKV=3T2

⇒KdVdT=3T2

⇒dV=3T2KdT

⇒γV=3T2Kas dV=γVdT

⇒γ=3T2KV=3T2T3=3T

(Given, mean kinetic energy of a molecule (at T) is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>−</mo><mn>14</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>erg</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>980</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> density of mercury <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>13</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>cm</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
question: Number of molecules in a volume of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><msup><mi>cm</mi><mn>3</mn></msup></math> of a perfect monoatomic gas at some temperature T and at a pressure of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> of mercury is close to? (Given, mean kinetic energy of a molecule (at T) is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>−</mo><mn>14</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>erg</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>980</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> density of mercury <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>13</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>cm</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
solution:
N=PVKT

U=32KT

∴  N=3PV2U=3.99×1018

subject: Physics
q: In an ideal gas at temperature T, the average force that a molecule applies on the walls of a closed container depends on <math><mi>T</mi><mi> </mi><mi>a</mi><mi>s</mi><mi> </mi><msup><mrow><mi>T</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>q</mi></mrow></msup></math> . A good estimate for q is:
question: In an ideal gas at temperature T, the average force that a molecule applies on the walls of a closed container depends on <math><mi>T</mi><mi> </mi><mi>a</mi><mi>s</mi><mi> </mi><msup><mrow><mi>T</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>q</mi></mrow></msup></math> . A good estimate for q is:
solution:
Average linear velocity for collision to occur

t=2du

Change in momentum in 1 collision

Δp=2 mu

∴     average force in collision

= Δpt

(u= root mean square speed)

=2 mu2d ×u

⇒    F∝u2

∴     u2 ∝T

⇒    F∝T

⇒   q=1

subject: Physics
q: Assuming the nitrogen molecule is moving with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi><mo>.</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>.</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>. velocity at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>400</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math>, the de-Broglie wave length of nitrogen molecule is close to : (Given : nitrogen molecule weight : <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo>.64</mo><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>−</mo><mn>26</mn></mrow></msup><mtext> </mtext><mi>kg</mi></math>, Boltzman constant : <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.38</mo><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>−</mo><mn>23</mn></mrow></msup><mtext> </mtext><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, Planck constant : <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo>.63</mo><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>−</mo><mn>34</mn></mrow></msup><mtext> </mtext><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>)
question: Assuming the nitrogen molecule is moving with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi><mo>.</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>.</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>. velocity at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>400</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math>, the de-Broglie wave length of nitrogen molecule is close to : (Given : nitrogen molecule weight : <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo>.64</mo><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>−</mo><mn>26</mn></mrow></msup><mtext> </mtext><mi>kg</mi></math>, Boltzman constant : <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.38</mo><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>−</mo><mn>23</mn></mrow></msup><mtext> </mtext><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, Planck constant : <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo>.63</mo><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>−</mo><mn>34</mn></mrow></msup><mtext> </mtext><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>)
solution:
λ=h2mk=hm×32kT=h3mkT

=6.63×10-343×4.64×10-26×1.38×10-13×400=0.24 A°

subject: Physics
q: Two ideal polyatomic gases at temperatures <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>T</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>T</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> are mixed so that there is no loss of energy. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>F</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>F</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><msub><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>m</mi></mrow><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>m</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><msub><mi>n</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>n</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> be the degrees of freedom, masses, number of molecules of the first and second gas respectively, the temperature of mixture of these two gases is:
question: Two ideal polyatomic gases at temperatures <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>T</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>T</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> are mixed so that there is no loss of energy. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>F</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>F</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><msub><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>m</mi></mrow><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>m</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><msub><mi>n</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>n</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> be the degrees of freedom, masses, number of molecules of the first and second gas respectively, the temperature of mixture of these two gases is:
solution:
Let the final temperature of the mixture be T.

Since, there is no loss in energy.

ΔU=0

⇒F12n1RΔT+F22n2RΔT=0

⇒F12n1RT1-T+F22n2RT2-T=0

⇒T=F1n1RT1+F2n2RT2 F1n1R+F2n2R=F1n1 T1+F2n2 T2 F1n1+F2n2

subject: Physics
q: There are two identical chambers, completely thermally insulated from surroundings. Both chambers have a partition wall dividing the chambers in two compartments. Compartment $1$ is filled with an ideal gas and Compartment $3$ is filled with a real gas. Compartments $2$ and $4$ are vacuum. A small hole (orifice) is made in the partition walls and the gases are allowed to expand in vacuum. $\mathrm{Statement-1:}$ No change in the temperature of the gas takes place when ideal gas expands in vacuum. However, the temperature of real gas goes down (cooling) when it expands in vacuum. $\mathrm{Statement-2:}$ The internal energy of an ideal gas is only kinetic. The internal energy of a real gas is kinetic as well as potential.
question: There are two identical chambers, completely thermally insulated from surroundings. Both chambers have a partition wall dividing the chambers in two compartments. Compartment $1$ is filled with an ideal gas and Compartment $3$ is filled with a real gas. Compartments $2$ and $4$ are vacuum. A small hole (orifice) is made in the partition walls and the gases are allowed to expand in vacuum. $\mathrm{Statement-1:}$ No change in the temperature of the gas takes place when ideal gas expands in vacuum. However, the temperature of real gas goes down (cooling) when it expands in vacuum. $\mathrm{Statement-2:}$ The internal energy of an ideal gas is only kinetic. The internal energy of a real gas is kinetic as well as potential.
options: Statement-1 is false and Statement-2 is true.

Statement-1 and Statement-2 both are true. 
Statement-2 is the correct explanation of Statement-1.
Statement-1 is true and Statement- 2 is false.

Statement-1 and Statement-2 both are true. 
Statement-2 is not correct explanation of Statement-1.
solution: In ideal gases the molecules are considered as point particles and for point particles, there is no internal excitation, no vibration and no rotation. For an ideal gas the internal energy can only be translational kinetic energy and for real gas both kinetic as well as potential energy.

subject: Physics
q: Two thermodynamical process are shown in the figure. The molar heat capacity for process A and B are CA and CB. The molar heat capacity at constant pressure and constant volume are represented by CP and CV, respectively. Choose the correct statement.
question: Two thermodynamical process are shown in the figure. The molar heat capacity for process A and B are CA and CB. The molar heat capacity at constant pressure and constant volume are represented by CP and CV, respectively. Choose the correct statement.
solution:
For process A:

logP=γlogV⇒P=Vγ,(γ>1)

PV-γ= Constant

CA=CV+R1+γ …i

Likewise, for process B→PV-1= Constant

CB=Cv+R1+1

CB=Cv+R2 …ii

Also, CP=Cv+R …iii

By ii and iii, we get

CP>CB>Cv

subject: Physics
q: An <math><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi></math> molecule has rotational, translational and vibrational motions. If the rms velocity of <math><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi></math> molecules in its gaseous phase is <math><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">ν</mi></mrow><mo>-</mo></mover></math> , m is its mass and <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></mrow></msub></math> is Boltzmann's constant, then its temperature will be:
question: An <math><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi></math> molecule has rotational, translational and vibrational motions. If the rms velocity of <math><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi></math> molecules in its gaseous phase is <math><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">ν</mi></mrow><mo>-</mo></mover></math> , m is its mass and <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></mrow></msub></math> is Boltzmann's constant, then its temperature will be:
solution:
Vrms=3kBTm 
v-=3kBTm
T=mv-23kB

subject: Physics
q: Two rigid boxes containing different ideal gases are placed on a table. Box A contains one mole of nitrogen at temperature $T_0$, while Box $B$ contains one mole of helium at temperature $(7 / 3) T_0$. The boxes are then put into thermal contact with each other and heat flows between them until the gases reach a common final temperature. (Ignore the heat capacity of boxes). Then, the final temperature of the gases, $T_f$, in terms of $T_0$ is
question: Two rigid boxes containing different ideal gases are placed on a table. Box A contains one mole of nitrogen at temperature $T_0$, while Box $B$ contains one mole of helium at temperature $(7 / 3) T_0$. The boxes are then put into thermal contact with each other and heat flows between them until the gases reach a common final temperature. (Ignore the heat capacity of boxes). Then, the final temperature of the gases, $T_f$, in terms of $T_0$ is
solution:
$\Delta \mathrm{U}=0$
$\Rightarrow \frac{3}{2} \mathrm{R}\left(\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{f}}-\mathrm{T}_0\right)+1 \times \frac{5}{2} \mathrm{R}\left(\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{f}}-\frac{7}{3} \mathrm{~T}_0\right)=0$
$\mathrm{~T}_{\mathrm{f}}=\frac{3}{2} \mathrm{~T}_0$

subject: Physics
q: The number of air molecules per cm3 is increased from 3×1019 to 12×1019. The ratio of collision frequency of air molecules before and after the increase in number respectively is :
question: The number of air molecules per cm3 is increased from 3×1019 to 12×1019. The ratio of collision frequency of air molecules before and after the increase in number respectively is :
solution:
The given data is 

n1=3×1019n2=12×1019

The mean time between two collision has the meaning of a period τ, since it indicates the repetitive time intervals in which on average collisions take place. The reciprocal of the time τ can be understood as the collision frequency f, which indicates the number of collisions per unit time.The collision frequency is also directly proportional to the number molecules per unit volume.

f∝1τ

⇒f∝n

⇒f1 f2 =n1n2=3×101912×1019=0.25

subject: Physics
q: The root mean square velocity of molecules of gas is
question: The root mean square velocity of molecules of gas is
options: Inversely proportional to square root of temperature (<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msqrt><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mi>T</mi></mfrac></msqrt></math>).
Proportional to square root of temperature <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msqrt><mi>T</mi></msqrt></math>.
solution:
Root mean speed of a gas molecule is stated as vrms=3RTM, where, R is the universal gas constant, T is temperature, and M is the molar mass of the gas.

Thus, vrms∝T.

subject: Physics
q: A gas of certain mass filled in a closed cylinder at a pressure of 3.23 kPa has temperature $50^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The gas is now heated to double its temperature. The modified pressure is $\_\_\_\_$ Pa. Note: Volume is constant.
question: A gas of certain mass filled in a closed cylinder at a pressure of 3.23 kPa has temperature $50^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The gas is now heated to double its temperature. The modified pressure is $\_\_\_\_$ Pa. Note: Volume is constant.
solution:
Since its given that volume is constant, $P \propto T$.

$T_i = 50°C = 323$ K
$T_f = 2 \times 50 = 100°C = 373$ K

$\frac{P_f}{P_i} = \frac{T_f}{T_i}$

$\frac{P_f}{3.23 \text{ kPa}} = \frac{373}{323}$

$P_f = 3.23 \times \frac{373}{323} = 3.73$ kPa $= 3730$ Pa

subject: Physics
q: A vessel contains <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>16</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of hydrogen and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>128</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of oxygen at standard temperature and pressure. The volume of the vessel in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>cm</mi><mn>3</mn></msup></math> is :
question: A vessel contains <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>16</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of hydrogen and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>128</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of oxygen at standard temperature and pressure. The volume of the vessel in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>cm</mi><mn>3</mn></msup></math> is :
solution:
Number of moles of Hydrogen H2=mM=162=8

Number of moles of oxygen O2=12832=4

Using ideal gas equation, PV=nRT,

Where pressure P=1 atm=1.01×105 Pa STP

Putting the values, 

1.01×105 V=8+4×8.314×273

V=12×8.314×2731.01×105 m3

=0.27×106 cm3=27×104 cm3

subject: Physics
q:
According to kinetic theory of gases,

A. The motion of the gas molecules freezes at 0°C.

B. The mean free path of gas molecules decreases if the density of molecules is increased.

C. The mean free path of gas molecules increases if temperature is increased keeping pressure constant.

D. Average kinetic energy per molecule per degree of freedom is 32kBT (for monoatomic gases).

Choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below
question:
According to kinetic theory of gases,

A. The motion of the gas molecules freezes at 0°C.

B. The mean free path of gas molecules decreases if the density of molecules is increased.

C. The mean free path of gas molecules increases if temperature is increased keeping pressure constant.

D. Average kinetic energy per molecule per degree of freedom is 32kBT (for monoatomic gases).

Choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below
solution:
According to kinetic theory of gases,

A. The motion of the gas molecules freezes at 0 K.  

B. The mean free path decreases on increasing the number density of the molecules.

C. The mean free path increases on increasing the volume. Now if temperature is increased by keeping the pressure constant the volume should increase that is mean free path increases. 

Mean free path λ=12πd2n, here, n=NV

⇒λ=12πd2NV

Using, PV=NKT

⇒λ=KT2πd2P 

D. Average kinetic energy per molecule per degree of freedom is 12kBT.

Thus, B and C only.

subject: Physics
q: A thermally insulated vessel contains an ideal gas of molecular mass $M$ and ratio of specific heats $\gamma$. It is moving with speed $v$ and is suddenly brought to rest. Assuming no heat is lost to the surroundings, its temperature increases by :
question: A thermally insulated vessel contains an ideal gas of molecular mass $M$ and ratio of specific heats $\gamma$. It is moving with speed $v$ and is suddenly brought to rest. Assuming no heat is lost to the surroundings, its temperature increases by :
solution:
$\mathrm{W}=\Delta \mathrm{U}$
$\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{mv}^2=\mathrm{nC}_{\mathrm{v}} \mathrm{dT}$
$=\frac{\mathrm{m}}{\mathrm{M}} \frac{\mathrm{R}}{\gamma-1} \mathrm{dT}$
$\mathrm{dT}=\frac{\mathrm{M}(\gamma-1) \mathrm{v}^2}{2 \mathrm{R}} \mathrm{K}$

subject: Physics
q: The volume <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>V</mi></math> of an enclosure contains a mixture of three gases, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>16</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of oxygen, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>28</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of nitrogen and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>44</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of carbon dioxide at absolute temperature <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>T</mi></math>. Consider <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> as universal gas constant. The pressure of the mixture of gases is :
question: The volume <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>V</mi></math> of an enclosure contains a mixture of three gases, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>16</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of oxygen, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>28</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of nitrogen and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>44</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of carbon dioxide at absolute temperature <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>T</mi></math>. Consider <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> as universal gas constant. The pressure of the mixture of gases is :
solution:
PV=n1+n2+n3RT

P×V=1632+2828+4444RT

PV=12+1+1RT

P=52RTV

subject: Physics
q:
The root mean square speed of smoke particles of mass 5×10-17 kg in their Brownian motion in air at NTP is approximately.

[Given k=1.38×10-23J K-1]
question:
The root mean square speed of smoke particles of mass 5×10-17 kg in their Brownian motion in air at NTP is approximately.

[Given k=1.38×10-23J K-1]
solution:
According to Kinetic theory, 

Average kinetic energy of a gas molecule =12mvrms2=32kT

Thus, vrms=3kTm

At NTP, T=293 K

Putting the values, we have

=3×1.38×10-23×2935×10-17

≈15 mm s-1

subject: Physics
q:
The total kinetic energy of 1 mole of oxygen at 27°C is :
[Use universal gas constant (R)=8.31 J mol-1 K-1 ]
question:
The total kinetic energy of 1 mole of oxygen at 27°C is :
[Use universal gas constant (R)=8.31 J mol-1 K-1 ]
solution:
The formula to calculate the kinetic energy can be written as

K=f2nRT   ...1

Since oxygen is a diatomic gas, the number of degrees of freedom is 5.

Thus, from equation (1), it follows that

K=52×1×8.31×300 J=6232.5 J

subject: Physics
q: An ideal gas is enclosed in a cylinder at pressure of <math><mn>2</mn></math> atm and temperature, <math><mn>300</mn><mi> </mi><mi>K</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The mean time between two successive collisions is <math><mn>6</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>8</mn></mrow></msup><mi>s</mi><mo>.</mo></math> If the pressure is doubled and temperature is increased to <math><mn>500</mn><mi> </mi><mi>K</mi><mo>,</mo></math> the mean time between two successive collisions will be close to:
question: An ideal gas is enclosed in a cylinder at pressure of <math><mn>2</mn></math> atm and temperature, <math><mn>300</mn><mi> </mi><mi>K</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The mean time between two successive collisions is <math><mn>6</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>8</mn></mrow></msup><mi>s</mi><mo>.</mo></math> If the pressure is doubled and temperature is increased to <math><mn>500</mn><mi> </mi><mi>K</mi><mo>,</mo></math> the mean time between two successive collisions will be close to:
solution:
τ=12nπd2 vavg

Where n= number of molecules per unit volume.

τ∝VT⇒τ∝TP

τ2τ1=T2T1.P1P2⇒τ2=6×10-8×5000300×24

≈4×10-8s

subject: Physics
q: At what temperature is the r.m.s. velocity of a hydrogen molecule equal to that of an oxygen molecule at $47^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ ?
question: At what temperature is the r.m.s. velocity of a hydrogen molecule equal to that of an oxygen molecule at $47^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ ?
solution: $\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{ms}} \alpha \sqrt{\mathrm{T} / \mathrm{m}} ; \quad \sqrt{\frac{273+47}{32}}=\sqrt{\frac{T}{2}}$ or $\mathrm{T}=20 \mathrm{~K}$

subject: Physics
q: The relation between root mean square speed <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><msub><mi>v</mi><mrow><mi>rms</mi><mo> </mo></mrow></msub></mfenced></math> and most probable speed <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><msub><mi>v</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">p</mi></msub></mfenced></math> for the molar mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>M</mi></math> of oxygen gas molecule at the temperature of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>300</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math> will be
question: The relation between root mean square speed <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><msub><mi>v</mi><mrow><mi>rms</mi><mo> </mo></mrow></msub></mfenced></math> and most probable speed <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><msub><mi>v</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">p</mi></msub></mfenced></math> for the molar mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>M</mi></math> of oxygen gas molecule at the temperature of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>300</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math> will be
solution:
RMS speed of O2 gas is vrms=3RTM=3RT32 and most probable speed of O2 gas is vmost probable=2RTM=2RT32.

Here temperature is same.

Thus, vrmsvmost probable=3RT322RT32=32

Hence, vrms=32vp

subject: Physics
q: The plot that depicts the behavior of the mean free time <math><mi>τ</mi></math> (time between two successive collisions) for the molecules of an ideal gas, as a function of temperature <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>T</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>,</mo></math> qualitatively, is: (Graphs are schematic and not drawn to scale)
question: The plot that depicts the behavior of the mean free time <math><mi>τ</mi></math> (time between two successive collisions) for the molecules of an ideal gas, as a function of temperature <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>T</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>,</mo></math> qualitatively, is: (Graphs are schematic and not drawn to scale)
solution:
The average time taken by a moving particle between successive collisions or impacts is referred to as the mean free time τ.

The average distance a moving particle travels between successive collisions or impacts is referred to as the mean free path λ.

λ=kT2πd2P

and vrms=3RTM

So, the mean free time τ=λvrms

Therefore, τ∝1T
